meta.title: Commercial Tableware for Indian HoReCa — Playable Case Study
meta.hubTagline: Own the premium-durable-serviced ceramic quadrant Indian HoReCa has no branded champion for — asset-light, private-label, and built on a recurring-replacement annuity.

hero.sub: A playable walk-through of the Commercial Tableware dossier. Same facts, same numbers — but you get to investigate, decide, and vote.

opening.lead: No branded Indian house owns the premium-durable-serviced ceramic HoReCa quadrant. Imports own the top, unbranded Khurja the bottom, and the middle is empty. That gap is the whole thesis. Here is the shape of it.

stats.labelHtml: Branded Indian houses owning the premium-durable-serviced ceramic HoReCa quadrant — the whitespace, quantified
New branded hotel rooms in India's pipeline to FY29 — a rolling wave of pre-opening fit-outs
Per-plate anti-dumping duty on Chinese ceramic tableware (USD 1,075/MT) — the import-substitution wedge EST.
Total capital to launch — mostly recyclable working capital, not a kiln EST.

imgband.title: Two bad options, one broken table
imgband.sub: Imports (Steelite, RAK, Villeroy & Boch) look right and last — but carry duty, container MOQs, long lead times and get discontinued. Local unbranded Khurja is cheap and fast but batch-inconsistent, so re-orders never match. Within 12–24 months every premium venue is serving on a chipped, mismatched table.

whitespace.lead: Ten real players, plotted. Hover any dot. Notice where nobody is: premium-priced and branded-plus-serviced. That corner — where you can re-supply the exact plate for years — is what this business is built to own.

players.d: UK category leader, no owned India entity
design-led HoReCa, closest to the gap
claims ~70% premium hotels, but a factory

whitespace.sidebarHtml:
Reading the map.

Imports like Steelite, RAK & V&B own the top on price and prestige — but are agent-distributed, duty-loaded and slow to re-supply. Indian makers (Clay Craft, Bharat Potteries, Feather Touch) have scale and 5-star clients but present as factories, not branded service. Ariane is closest — yet still hasn't fused kiln-cost, import-grade design and a held-SKU guarantee.

The top-right — premium and serviced — is uncontested. That is the investable idea.

hook.lead: Anyone who pitches this deal on “proprietary designs” is selling the wrong story. Real diligence ranked every candidate moat by defensibility. Click each case file to see the verdict — and watch the story flip.
hook.statusInitialTail: open them all to close the case.
hook.statusSolvedHtml: Case closed. The plate is not the moat — the re-supply is. Design IP, brand-as-spec and re-order lock-in all fail as standalone walls. The boring one — distribution, held inventory, service and colour-batch continuity — is the only durable moat. Steelite paying up for Utopia's distribution engine in 2024 (not its kilns) is the proof.

cases.name: Proprietary plate designs
cases.det: A Khurja kiln can copy any plate we design within a season. Design is low defensibility as law, medium as a capability — a season-ahead treadmill, not a wall. Owning the finishing step is the only defensible slice.

cases.name: Brand-as-spec
cases.det: “Brand” has historically been weak leverage in B2B crockery. It's 5–10-year option value at best — N/A at launch, and it cannot be built directly. The premium must be earned by service + total cost of ownership, not a logo.

cases.name: Re-order lock-in
cases.det: Real economics — but a derivative amplifier. It only accrues to us through the boring moat below. Remove the held-SKU guarantee and it collapses to a one-time commodity sale.

cases.name: Chip warranty
cases.det: A powerful trust moat — but gated. A durability promise on kilns you don't own is a put option written against your own supply chain. Bound it (2-year, named range), underwrite it with QC data, and it holds.

cases.name: Held inventory + service
cases.clue: “Just a warehouse and a service desk.”
cases.det: The real moat. Holding graded stock for years, colour-batch-matching a 2027 plate to 2024 originals, and shipping 40 replacements in days is working-capital-heavy logistics a job-shop kiln does not want and an importer cannot do inside a container MOQ.

personas.lead: A committee sale with a split buyer — the emotional buyer and the economic buyer are different people. Every persona has a trigger, an objection, and one thing that converts them. Flip each card to see what wins the account.

items.name: Aditi, Executive Chef
items.role: New Mumbai fine-diner · the emotional buyer
items.trig: A distinctive reactive-glaze coupe that makes her plating sing — and a new outlet opening that needs a full opening set.
items.obj: Falling in love with an import range that gets discontinued, or a local batch that comes back a different green.
items.conv: A held-SKU guarantee — same shape, same glaze, colour-matched for years — backed by owned finishing and a bounded chip warranty.

items.name: Rakesh, F&B Purchase Manager
items.trig: An RFQ for a pre-opening fit-out; an ESG / made-in-India sourcing mandate.
items.obj: Stock-outs mid-season, re-tendering a 6-plate breakage order, and warranty claims that don't get honoured.
items.conv: Empanelment, landed cost below imports, fast small-order replacement, plus FSSAI and lead/cadmium-free certificates.

items.name: Priya, F&B Consultant
items.role: Kitchen planner on new-builds · the gatekeeper
items.trig: Drafting the crockery spec and vendor shortlist for a greenfield hotel or restaurant.
items.obj: Recommending an untested new brand puts her reputation on the line; she needs proven references.
items.conv: Two to three lighthouse 5-star references + a QC-backed bounded warranty + a Mumbai showroom and sample library she can send clients to.

items.name: Vikram, GM / Owner-Promoter
items.trig: Signing off a large fit-out; protecting a ₹2,000+ dining experience from a degrading tabletop.
items.obj: Needs bulk reliability and a total cost of ownership below imports across a multi-year outlet life.
items.conv: The razor/annuity math — below-import landed cost on the opening order, plus a re-supply annuity that keeps the tabletop intact for a decade.

tycoon.lead: Four real decisions from the dossier. Each has a right answer the diligence already argues for. Pick — then see what the evidence says.

decisions.ctx: La Opala owns its kilns and prints ~37% operating margins. Tempting. Pick your asset base.

opts.t: Build a kiln to capture the manufacturing margin
opts.fb: That's a ₹20–50cr+ capex bet, and it forfeits the entire thesis. The whole edge is asset-light: recyclable working capital, not a sunk factory. La Opala's 37% belongs to a kiln-owner — you would be copying the wrong template.

opts.t: Asset-light brand on kilns that already exist
opts.fb: Correct. The Utopia template. Clay Craft advertises OEM/private-label and Bharat Potteries runs 70M+ pieces a year — hungry for branded demand. The kiln is a vendor, not a balance-sheet asset.

decisions.q: 2 · How do you write the warranty?
decisions.ctx: The chip warranty is your headline — but you don't own the kiln. Choose the promise.

opts.fb: A durability promise on kilns you don't control is a put option written against your own supply chain. Over-promise and every batch defect becomes a warranty liability you can't underwrite.

opts.t: Bounded 2-year warranty on a named range, widened as data proves the body
opts.fb: Correct. Underwrite the claim with incoming QC and batch testing, treat kiln consistency as a gate to clear before scaling the warranty — an honest, defensible trust moat.

decisions.q: 3 · How do you source to keep the promise?
decisions.ctx: The durability promise means you can't spot-buy blindly across rotating kilns. Choose your supply discipline.

opts.t: Spot-buy from whichever kiln is cheapest each order
opts.fb: That reintroduces exactly the odd-plate, batch-mismatch problem you're selling against. Cheapest-per-order destroys the colour-batch continuity that IS the moat.

opts.t: Anchor-kiln dedicated lines + own the finishing step
opts.fb: Correct. Concentrate volume with 1–2 quality partners, dual-source hero SKUs, and own decoration/finishing for design IP + QC control — exactly the layer Steelite and Utopia own.

decisions.ctx: The single most important framing in the deck. Anchor the model on the right comp.

opts.fb: Wrong comp — that belongs to a vertically-integrated kiln-owner. As an asset-light reseller you forfeit the manufacturing slice; any model assuming 37% is broken.

opts.t: ~45–55% gross, ~10–20% operating — benchmarked to Churchill China's ~10–12%
opts.fb: Correct. The honest asset-light landing zone. Churchill China's ~10–12% manufacturer margin is the sensible floor-to-mid reference; you earn a resale mark-up on far less capital.

human.titleHtml: The most important ₹ in the deck.
human.lead: Take one 80-cover Mumbai fine-diner. The opening fit-out is a one-time ~₹7.8 lakh order. But at ~15%/yr breakage, the replacement “blades” exceed that opening order within ~6–7 years — and over a 10-year account life the annuity is bigger than the acquisition sale.
human.note: Remove the held-SKU guarantee and this collapses to a one-time commodity sale. The moat and the annuity are the same fact viewed twice: whoever holds your exact SKU and re-supplies it plate-for-plate captures the stream by default — because re-ordering from anyone else re-introduces the mismatch problem. All figures EST., to be replaced with a real Mumbai fine-dining spec sheet in diligence.

quiz.lead: Five questions. Then you cast the vote you'd cast in the room.

opts: Ceramic tableware is a brand-new product in India
No branded Indian house owns the premium-durable-serviced ceramic HoReCa quadrant
items.e: Imports own the top but are agent-distributed and slow; Indian makers have scale but present as factories; Khurja owns price and nothing else. The premium-durable-serviced quadrant has no branded Indian champion.

items.q: Why is the moat the “boring” one, not proprietary designs?
opts: Designs are illegal to trademark in India
A kiln can copy any plate; the defensible moat is held inventory + service + colour-batch continuity
Because customers don't care about design
items.e: The plate is not the moat; the re-supply is. Holding graded stock for years and colour-batch-matching a replacement is working-capital-heavy logistics a kiln won't do. Steelite bought Utopia for distribution, not kilns.

items.q: Why not anchor the model on La Opala's ~37% operating margin?
opts: That margin belongs to a vertically-integrated kiln-owner; asset-light targets ~10–20% (Churchill ~10–12%)
items.e: As an asset-light reseller you forfeit the manufacturing slice. The realistic landing zone is ~45–55% gross and ~10–20% operating at scale — benchmarked to Churchill China's ~10–12% manufacturer margin.

items.q: What is the gating technical milestone the plan must clear?
opts: Reactive-glaze batch consistency on kilns it doesn't own
items.e: The chip-warranty differentiator is in direct tension with the asset-light model. If 1–2 anchor kilns can't hit reactive-glaze batch-match in the pilot, the warranty collapses — re-scope or stop.

items.q: What happens if the anti-dumping duty lapses in ~May 2027?
opts: Import prices could fall ~₹30–40/plate; the ADD is a tailwind, not the thesis
items.e: The ADD (USD 1,075/MT, ~₹40/plate) needs a fresh DGTR sunset review. Model a no-ADD downside — but the ~40% BCD/duty stack and the service/continuity moat remain regardless.

vote.prompt: You've seen the whitespace, the moat trap, the split buyer, the unit economics and the annuity. What's your call?
vote.noLabel: Pass — the cash trap is the moat
vote.yesMsg: Backed. Your Phase-1 first move (per the Action Plan): pull 3–4 real distributor quotes on a comparable 27cm plate (RAK via EFF N Bee, Bonna via Polymech, Clay Craft direct), benchmark Ariane / Feather Touch / Clay Craft hard, run 5–8 primary Mumbai chef/F&B interviews, and pilot anchor-kiln batch consistency — all inside Months 0–6. Patient, working-capital-disciplined capital, not venture-scale.
vote.noMsg: Passed — and defensibly so. The dossier agrees the variance is real: this is not greenfield (Ariane, Feather Touch and Clay Craft already hold parts of the thesis), the product is copyable, the chip-warranty headline is in direct tension with an asset-light model, brand is weak leverage in B2B crockery, the moat is itself a working-capital cash trap, and the ADD tailwind lapses in 2027. A pass here is a legitimate IC position — the case study never pretends otherwise.

footer.note1: This playable walk-through is a companion to the full institutional dossier — it simplifies for engagement and never overrides the source document.
footer.note2: Every number here is drawn verbatim from the dossier, including its EST. flags and unverified-source caveats.
